extra.reasoning:
This is a state machine. The safety property is globally(opted_out implies not sent): in every reachable state with opted_out true, sent must be false.

The only transitions that set sent := true are t2 (send_attempt when not opted_out, harmless) and t4 (send_attempt when opted_out and acc == 32). t4 is the danger: if opted_out is true AND acc == 32, a send_attempt sets sent := true while opted_out is true, violating the property.

The step transitions (t5/t6/t7) compute acc := (3*acc + c) mod 64 with c = 32, 18, 23 respectively (the nested if-then-else chains simply subtract 64 up to three times, reducing modulo 64; max pre-reduction value is 3*63+32 = 221, needing at most three subtractions). The optout event (t1) can fire at any time and step events ignore opted_out, so acc can be driven to any reachable value after opting out.

From initial acc = 0, one step_0 gives acc = (3*0 + 32) mod 64 = 32. Therefore acc = 32 with opted_out = true is reachable in two events.

Concrete 3-event counterexample (within the 66-event bound):
1. optout (t1): opted_out=true, sent=false, acc=0
2. step_0 (t5): acc=(3*0+32) mod 64=32, sent=false; state opted_out=true, sent=false, acc=32
3. send_attempt: t2 disabled (opted_out true), t3 disabled (acc==32 so acc!=32 is false), t4 enabled (opted_out and acc==32) -> sent:=true; state opted_out=true, sent=true, acc=32

Final state has opted_out=true and sent=true, violating globally(opted_out implies not sent). Verified by direct simulation of the transition semantics. Verdict: violated.
